fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship that API response toolkit we've been talking about. The idea is pretty straightforward — devs are sick of hand-rolling the same JSON envelope boilerplate every time they build an endpoint. We need something that handles wrapping successful payloads, dealing with errors, and all the pagination stuff (both the page-number kind and the cursor-based one we did for the feed feature). 

Also important: there's some validation logic around HTTP status ranges — like you can't return a 'success' wrapper with a 400-level code, that kind of thing. We burned ourselves on that before. Make sure that's locked down.

The response object itself needs to be inspectable — clients downstream (like our test harness) need to be able to peek at the status, headers, body, and the raw JSON string separately. There's also that sparse fields thing the frontend team keeps asking about — they only want specific keys back, and if the resource label is missing it should fail gracefully, not silently.

Oh and remember how we handled the side-loaded relations on the articles endpoint? Same pattern here — strip the envelope off each relation before attaching it. The decorator chain behavior should follow what we agreed on in the last architecture sync. Ping me if unclear, but basically status and success flags get prepended in order.

trigger_keywords: success response status range
which HTTP codes are valid for success
what status codes can a success response use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Success responses only accept HTTP status codes in the range 100–399 inclusive. Any status code from 400 onwards will be rejected with the normalized error output: `error=invalid_status`.

trigger_keywords: error response status range
which HTTP codes are valid for error
what status codes can an error response use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Error responses only accept HTTP status codes in the range 400–599 inclusive. Any status code below 400 will be rejected with the normalized error output: `error=invalid_status`.

trigger_keywords: how does pagination URL construction work
how are previous and next links built for page pagination
page link URL format with query params
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For page-number pagination, previous and next URLs are constructed by appending the existing query parameters to the path, then appending `&page=<n>`. For example, with path `http://api.test/articles`, query `{filter: recent}`, and currentPage 2 of 3, the output is: `previous: http://api.test/articles?filter=recent&page=1`, `next: http://api.test/articles?filter=recent&page=3`. totalPages is computed as `ceil(total / perPage)`.

trigger_keywords: how are included relations merged
side-loaded resource envelope stripping
how to unwrap included data before attaching to primary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each included relation is stored under a `data` envelope (e.g., `{ "author": { "data": { ... } } }`). Before merging into the primary data object, the `data` key must be unwrapped so only its value is attached directly under the relation name. The final merged object contains the primary fields plus each relation name mapped to its unwrapped value.

trigger_keywords: decorator order for response body
how are status and success fields prepended
body decorator chain application order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Decorators are applied in the order they appear in the `decorators` array and prepend fields to the body in that order. For decorators `["status", "success"]` on a 201 response with `data: {foo:1}`, the resulting body is `{"status":201,"success":true,"data":{"foo":1}}`. The `status` decorator adds the HTTP status integer, and the `success` decorator adds a boolean `true`.

trigger_keywords: what fields does the framework response expose
what does the inspectable response object contain
how do I read status headers body and content from a response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The framework JSON response object exposes four fields: `status` (integer HTTP status code), `headers` (object of response headers), `body` (decoded JSON object), and `content` (raw JSON string of the body). The `content` field is a JSON-encoded string of the body object, so it must itself be JSON-escaped when embedded in the outer response JSON.

trigger_keywords: sparse fieldset missing resource key error
what happens if no resource key is provided with fieldsets
error when fieldsets are requested without a resource label
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When sparse fieldsets are requested but no `resourceKey` is provided, the output must be the two-line normalized error: `error=missing_resource_key\nfeature=sparse_fieldsets\n`. This is not a JSON response — it is plain text key=value lines.

trigger_keywords: how does sparse field filtering work
which fields are returned when fieldsets are provided
sparse fields with a resource key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a `resourceKey` and `fieldsets` array are both provided, only the fields listed in `fieldsets` are retained from the item data. The output is wrapped in a `data` envelope: `{"data": { ...filtered fields... }}`. Fields not in `fieldsets` are dropped silently.

trigger_keywords: what does the http exception object look like
unauthenticated exception fields
domain http exception neutral contract structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A domain HTTP exception is represented as a plain object with the following fields: `error` (always the string `"http_exception"`), `status` (integer HTTP status, e.g. 401 for unauthenticated), `code` (machine-readable string, e.g. `"unauthenticated"`), `message` (nullable string), `data` (nullable), and `headers` (object, defaults to `{}`). For the `unauthenticated` type, status is 401 and code is `"unauthenticated"`.

trigger_keywords: cursor paginator fields
what does cursor_paginator output
cursor pagination state structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cursor paginator output contains: `current` (current cursor reference), `previous` (previous cursor reference), `next` (next cursor reference), `items` (the raw array of items passed in), and `collection` (also the array of items — same value as `items`). All five fields are present in the JSON output.

trigger_keywords: resource key when no data is available
what is resourceKey when dataType is none
resource key resolution for empty or unknown data
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no data source category can be matched (dataType is `"none"` or unrecognized), the resource key resolves to `null`. The output is `{"resourceKey":null}`.

trigger_keywords: how is null data classified
resource type for null input
classify null resource
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Null data is classified as a `"null"` resource type. The output includes `resourceType: "null"`, the provided `resourceKey`, and `data: null`. Scalar-object data is classified as `"item"`, and arrays of non-scalar objects are classified as `"collection"`.

trigger_keywords: error payload structure
how are additional fields included in error envelope
what goes inside the error object
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The error payload is wrapped in a top-level `error` object containing `code`, `message`, and any additional fields from the `data` input merged directly (not nested) into the error object. For example, `data: {foo: 1}` results in `{"error": {"code": "...", "message": "...", "foo": 1}}`.

trigger_keywords: laravel json response adapter output
framework response body and content fields
what does laravel_json_response return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `laravel_json_response` operation produces an object with `status` (integer), `headers` (object), `body` (decoded JSON object — the raw data passed in, not re-wrapped), and `content` (raw JSON string of the body). Unlike `build_success_response`, there is no decorator chain or data envelope applied — the data is used directly as the body.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The HTTP status category validation logic in Feature 6: success responses accept 100–399, error responses accept 400–599, implemented in the status validator module of the core domain

context_pointers.vague_reference: how we handled the side-loaded relations on the articles endpoint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 included resource merging: unwrap the `data` envelope from each relation in the `included` map before attaching it to the primary data object by relation name

context_pointers.vague_reference: what we agreed on in the last architecture sync about the decorator chain
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 decorator application order: decorators listed in the `decorators` array are applied in sequence, prepending `status` (integer) and `success` (boolean true) fields to the response body before the `data` key

Feature 7 cursor paginator: exposes current, previous, next cursor references plus items and collection arrays; resolution of cursor names requires an optional resolver and emits a normalized error without one

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here. Please check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature — each file contains the exact input/output contract for that operation. If it's still unclear, let me know which feature number you're looking at and I can try to clarify the business intent.

feature10_sparse_field_transforming.json.description: Transform item data with sparse fieldsets keyed by the resource type; nested requested relationships must remain available in parent and child fieldsets.

feature11_http_exception_contract.json.description: Represent domain HTTP exceptions as neutral error response data with status, error code, optional message, optional data, and headers.

feature12_framework_json_response_adapte.description: Create framework JSON responses that expose body content, status code, and headers through the framework response object.

feature1_success_payload_serialization.j.description: Serialize successful resource payloads using the default envelope for collections, single items, null resources, and metadata blocks.

feature2_success_pagination_serializatio.description: Serialize successful pagination metadata for page-number and cursor-based pagination without changing the resource data envelope.

feature3_included_resource_merging.json.description: Merge side-loaded included resources into transformed data by removing each included resource envelope before attaching it by relation name.

feature4_error_payload_serialization.jso.description: Serialize error payloads with an error envelope containing a code, a message, and any additional structured fields.

feature5_json_response_building.json.description: Build JSON HTTP responses that preserve response body data, HTTP status codes, selected headers, and optional body decorators.

feature6_status_validation_errors.json.description: Reject HTTP status codes that do not match the response category being built and report the error as a normalized category.

feature7_cursor_paginator.json.description: Expose cursor pagination references and paginated items, allow replacing items, and resolve cursor names through an optional resolver.

feature8_resource_key_resolution.json.description: Resolve resource keys from explicit bindings, the first item in a list, model-provided resource names, model table names, or return null when no key can be inferred.

feature9_resource_classification.json.description: Classify normalized data as a null resource, a single item resource, or a collection resource while retaining the resource key and data.
